behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is in the grouping/aggregation algorithm itself — a map/dict keyed by trace ID. This logic is entirely self-contained and can be exercised by passing in a list of log entry objects from different services. No module boundary handoff is required for the bug to surface. REAL-INFRA: No OS or network needed. LLM-DEP: None. The bug would show up as entries under the wrong key or split across multiple groups — catchable with a pure in-memory unit test.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a list of 6 log entry objects: 3 from service-A and 3 from service-B, interleaved, with two distinct trace IDs shared across services. Call the grouping function and assert that the result has exactly 2 groups, each containing exactly 3 entries, with no entry appearing in the wrong group.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode lives entirely in the sort/comparison logic — how the tool orders entries when their raw timestamps overlap due to skew. A real bug here (e.g., naive wall-clock sort that doesn't apply skew correction) is fully exposed by feeding a constructed list of timestamped entries. No inter-module handoff is needed. REAL-INFRA: None. LLM-DEP: None. The sorting function is deterministic and fully testable in isolation.
behaviors.test_description: Build a set of log entries with deliberate out-of-order timestamps simulating up to 499ms of clock skew between two services. Feed them to the timeline-ordering function and assert the returned sequence matches the logically correct causal order (not just raw timestamp order). Also include an entry where skew alone would swap two entries and verify it is correctly placed.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is in the statistical computation — the p99 calculation, baseline derivation, and threshold comparison. All of this is deterministic arithmetic that can be driven with hand-crafted latency arrays. No inter-module interaction is needed to expose a wrong percentile calculation or an off-by-one in the spike threshold. REAL-INFRA: None. LLM-DEP: None — this is rule-based anomaly detection, not an ML model. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing to mock.
behaviors.test_description: Construct two latency datasets: (1) a baseline distribution (e.g., 1000 values uniformly between 50ms–150ms) and (2) a test window containing 99 normal values plus one extreme outlier at 400ms (>3x the ~100ms baseline p99). Call the anomaly detector and assert the spike is flagged. Also provide a control dataset where p99 is 2.9x baseline and assert no flag is raised.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is in the per-entry parsing error handler — specifically whether the pipeline catches the JSON decode exception and increments the skip counter rather than propagating the crash. This is testable at the boundary of a single parse-and-dispatch function; no cross-module state is required. REAL-INFRA: None. LLM-DEP: None. The pipeline's behavior with bad input is fully exercised by feeding a mixed list of valid and invalid raw strings in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Pass a batch of 10 raw log strings to the pipeline entry point: 7 valid JSON objects with correct schema, 2 syntactically invalid JSON strings, and 1 valid JSON with a missing required field. Assert the pipeline returns without raising, the output contains exactly 7 processed entries, and the skip report shows a count of 3 with per-entry error context.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: A throughput failure at 10k entries/second is caused by real CPU, memory allocation, GC pressure, I/O scheduling, or queue backpressure — none of which are visible when the pipeline is mocked or run on a small synthetic workload. REAL-INFRA: Real OS scheduling and wall-clock timing are required to observe whether the pipeline falls behind under genuine load. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking any stage of the pipeline would conceal the actual throughput bottleneck. An integration test with a small batch would also pass trivially. The test must drive the real pipeline at the target rate for a sustained window and assert zero drops and processing lag below threshold.
behaviors.test_description: Spin up the full pipeline process (subprocess or in-process with real I/O path). Feed a pre-generated corpus of 30,000 valid log entries at a rate of 10,000/second using a real-time pacing loop. After the burst, assert: (1) all 30,000 entries appear in the output with no duplicates, (2) no entries are dropped (output count equals input count), and (3) the processing lag (time from last input entry to last output entry) stays under 1 second. Run on representative hardware or CI with resource constraints matched to production.
